facts_disclosed: DDS UCLA, residency USC, 3 years of 80-hour weeks

turns.content: I have been asked to serve on a mentorship panel for pre-dental students at a university here in San Diego. I want to be candid about what the path looks like without discouraging them. How would you suggest I structure brief remarks — perhaps five minutes — that are both honest and inspiring?

That's a great opportunity, and the balance you're describing — honest but not discouraging — is exactly what pre-dental students need to hear. They get plenty of glossy brochures; they rarely get someone telling them the truth with genuine care.

For five minutes, I'd suggest three beats: the decision, the difficulty, and the reward. Keep each one tight and anchor it in your own experience. Can you tell me a bit about your own path into dentistry? That'll help me shape the structure around your story.

Of course. I earned my DDS at UCLA. My father — he was also a dentist — drove me to the UCLA campus when I was twelve years old. He parked the car, pointed at the dental school building, and said, 'This is where you will study.' There was no discussion, jan. It was simply declared.

That's an incredible opening anecdote — a twelve-year-old standing in front of a building being told his future. It's vivid, it's personal, and it immediately establishes both the weight of expectation and the family legacy.

For the panel, I'd open with exactly that story. It accomplishes two things: it shows the students that paths into dentistry come in many forms (some chosen, some inherited), and it humanizes you immediately.

After UCLA, did you go straight into practice, or did you specialize?

I specialized. After UCLA I completed my periodontal residency at USC. That was three years of working eighty hours a week, sometimes more. The clinical load was relentless. I remember weeks where I slept in the residents' lounge more than in my own bed. It was the most demanding period of my life, without question.

That's the "difficulty" beat, and it's essential for these students to hear. Pre-dental students often fixate on getting into dental school and don't think much about what comes after — especially if they're considering specialization.

Three years of 80-hour weeks at USC is the kind of concrete detail that cuts through vague warnings about "hard work." I'd encourage you to be specific on the panel: the hours, the sleep deprivation, the moments of doubt. Students remember specifics; they forget generalities.

Here's how I'd structure your five minutes:

1. **The spark** (1 min) — Your father, UCLA at twelve. The origin of the calling, whether it was chosen or given to you.
2. **The grind** (2 min) — UCLA dental school, the USC residency, 80-hour weeks. Be specific about what it cost you. Don't sanitize it.
3. **The payoff** (2 min) — What practicing as a periodontist actually feels like now. The satisfaction of the work, building your own practice, the moments that make it worth everything you invested.

Would you like me to help draft any of these sections?

I would like help with the second section — the grind, as you put it. I want to convey the difficulty without making it sound like suffering for its own sake. There was purpose in every one of those hours, even when I could not see it at the time.

That distinction — purposeful difficulty versus pointless suffering — is exactly the right message. Here's a draft:

---

*"After dental school at UCLA, I went to USC for a three-year periodontal residency. I want to be straightforward with you about what that looked like: eighty-hour weeks were the norm, not the exception. There were stretches where I slept more in the residents' lounge than in my own apartment. The clinical demands were constant — you are learning to perform surgery on real patients, and there is no room for approximation.

But I want to be clear about something. Those hours were not wasted and they were not punishment. Every procedure I struggled through, every night I spent reviewing cases, built the hands and the judgment I rely on every single day in my practice. You cannot shortcut your way to competence in this profession. The difficulty is the training."*

---

This acknowledges the reality without glorifying exhaustion, and it reframes the hardship as investment. How does that feel?

That is very well done. The final line — 'the difficulty is the training' — I will absolutely use that. It captures precisely what I want them to understand. My only concern is that I do not want to seem as though I am boasting about suffering. These students already feel enough pressure.

That's a thoughtful concern. To avoid the "back in my day" tone, add a moment of vulnerability. Something like:

*"There were moments during residency when I questioned whether I had made the right choice. That is normal. If any of you feel that doubt during your training, it does not mean you are failing — it means the work is real."*

Admitting doubt does two things: it makes you relatable rather than intimidating, and it gives students permission to struggle without interpreting it as a sign they don't belong. The strongest mentors are the ones who say "I found it hard too" rather than "I powered through."

Would you also like to touch on why you chose periodontics specifically? That might help students who are trying to figure out whether to specialize or go into general practice.

That is a good idea, though I think I will keep it brief. Periodontics appealed to me because it is where precision matters most. The margin for error in soft tissue surgery is measured in fractions of a millimeter. That level of exactness — it suits my temperament. My wife would say it suits it too well, jan.

Ha — that's a charming line, and if you deliver it with a bit of self-awareness, it'll get a laugh from the audience. Humor in a mentorship talk is disarming in the best way.

The precision angle is also a useful signal for students: different specialties attract different personalities. Telling them "this suited my temperament" implicitly encourages them to think about their own temperament when choosing a path.

So your final structure would be:

1. **The spark** — Father, UCLA at twelve, the declaration
2. **The grind** — USC residency, 80-hour weeks, purposeful difficulty, admitting doubt
3. **Why periodontics** — Brief, precision and temperament, the light self-deprecating line
4. **The payoff** — What it all led to, the satisfaction of practicing on your own terms

Five minutes, tight and personal. Do you want help with the closing section, or do you have a sense of how you'd like to land it?

I think I know how I want to close. I want to bring it back to my father. He made the journey from having nothing in a new country to watching his son graduate from the very school he pointed to when I was twelve. That is the payoff. Not the degree on the wall, jan — the look on his face.

That is a perfect closing. The image of your father watching you graduate from the school he chose for you when you were twelve — it brings the entire story full circle. The students will remember that moment long after they forget any advice about study habits or board exams.

Deliver that last line simply and without rushing. Let it land.

You have a strong five minutes here: personal, honest, structured, and it ends on something deeply human rather than a list of tips. Those students are lucky to have you on the panel. Good luck with it.

Thank you. You have helped me organize what was a collection of scattered thoughts into something coherent and meaningful. I am looking forward to the panel now, which I was not an hour ago. I appreciate your help, jan.

You're very welcome. The thoughts weren't scattered — they were all there, just waiting for a structure. Everything you shared was vivid and genuine, which is why it came together quickly.

One last practical note: if any students approach you after the panel to ask about the periodontics path specifically, consider exchanging contact information. The best mentorship often happens in the follow-up conversations, not the panel itself. Enjoy it.
